short_summary: Unable to generate PDF invoices after recent platform upgrade
customer_problem: Since the v4.2.1 upgrade (released 2024‑05‑28), the “Export as PDF” button on the Invoicing page returns a generic 500‑Internal‑Server error. The error appears for all invoice records, regardless of size or status. The customer has verified that they are using a supported browser (Chrome 124) and that their user role has the required permissions.
business_impact: The customer (Acme Corp) processes ~2,500 invoices per week. Inability to export PDFs halts their month‑end closing process, forces manual data entry into a third‑party accounting system, and risks delayed payments to vendors. The issue is causing a projected revenue impact of $12,000 – $15,000 per week for the customer and is likely to increase churn risk if not resolved quickly.
customer_requested_action: Please investigate the root cause of the PDF generation failure, provide a fix or a temporary workaround (e.g., CSV export), and restore the PDF export functionality within the next 24 hours. If a fix will take longer, we need a clear ETA and a mitigation plan so the customer can continue processing invoices.
important_context: Upgrade logs show the PDF generation micro‑service (pdf‑gen‑svc) was redeployed with a new library version (libpdf v3.5).
The customer’s API key was rotated on 2024‑05‑30; logs show the key is still valid for other endpoints.
Error logs (see attached screenshot) contain the stack trace: `java.lang.NullPointerException at com.acme.pdf.Renderer.render(Renderer.java: 112)`.
The issue started immediately after the upgrade; prior to that PDF exports worked without incident for 18 months.
The customer has a Service Level Agreement (SLA) of 4‑hour response and 24‑hour resolution for “Critical – Business‑Impacting” incidents.
missing_information: Exact version of the PDF generation library that was bundled with the upgrade (release notes only mention “v3.5 – minor bug fixes”).
Whether any custom PDF templates have been applied by the customer (they may have overridden default templates).
Full stack trace from the server logs (the screenshot only shows the top 5 lines).
Confirmation that no other background jobs were running at the time of the failure (e.g., nightly batch jobs that might lock resources).
